narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical 3.21 average among past students—the law school admissions model took a close look at your full profile to predict your chances of passing the bar exam on the first try. Your LSAT score of 24, however, stands out as notably lower than the average of around 37, and this ended up being the single biggest factor pushing the model's prediction toward expecting you might fail the bar exam. Overall, the model estimates a 51% probability that you would fail on your first attempt, which is just over the threshold that led to your application's rejection, as the school prioritizes candidates predicted to pass with high confidence. The next most influential factor in your case was your undergraduate GPA, which worked strongly in your favor by pulling the prediction toward success, showing the model sees real promise in your college-level academic record. Similarly, your Year 3 school decile ranking of 4—better than the average of about 5.5—also nudged things positively, indicating your performance improved over time and compares favorably to many peers. Other details, like your middle-income family background and choice to attend part-time (uncommon, as over 90% go full-time), played smaller roles but didn't outweigh the LSAT's heavy drag. In essence, while your GPA and later-year grades position you ahead of typical students in those areas, the low LSAT score dominated the decision, tipping the scales just enough against you compared to applicants whose test scores align better with strong bar passage rates. This isn't a judgment on your potential as a lawyer—many successful ones have overcome similar hurdles—but the model's focus on first-time bar success makes that score a gatekeeper. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost it closer to average levels, as even a modest improvement could flip your predicted odds dramatically. You might also gather letters from professors highlighting your GPA strength and Year 3 progress to supplement future applications. Exploring part-time programs that value your unique path could align better with your plans, and some schools offer conditional admits with bar passage support. Keep building on your academic momentum; with targeted prep, you're well-positioned to reapply stronger next cycle.
